LCC Reviews.
← View portfolio
 Social media analytics · Trustpilot · 4 LCCs

The angriest LCC complaints are surprise baggage fees — and they're the cheapest to fix.

Clustered 4,368 negative reviews of four budget airlines into complaint types. Sentiment collapses into two statistical groups — and the harshest one is in-person fee friction, which costs almost nothing to address.

6,460
reviews collected
4,368
negative (< −0.3)
4
complaint clusters
UMAP of review embeddings K = 4
−0.79 worst ▼ harshest cluster sentiment
384-d
MiniLM embed
4
clusters
0.049
silhouette

Problem

Low-cost carriers collect a lot of bad reviews, but their model depends on keeping costs low — so "improve everything" is off the table. The real question is prioritization: which complaints hurt the most, and which of those can be fixed cheaply?

Goal

Group negative reviews into complaint types, measure how harsh each type is, and find the target that is high in sentiment intensity but low in cost to solve — the decision being where each airline should spend its limited service budget first.

Data

Two review sites, scraped with Selenium

Reviews labeled by airline across four budget carriers — Wizz, Ryanair, AirAsia, Scoot.

Trustpilot — adopted

Trustpilot.pkl · 6,460 reviews

General consumer review platform. The final clustering runs on this single source — cleaner and more consistent in style.

Skytrax — tested, dropped

Skytrax.pkl

Airline-specific platform. Merging it in degraded cluster quality — the two sites' review styles differ too much — so single-source won.

Method

Sentiment to find complaints, embeddings to group them

Every review scored with VADER; only reviews below −0.3 kept as genuine complaints → 4,368 reviews. Then two clustering approaches, baseline first.

Approach A — TF-IDF + K-means

Sparse word-count vectors. The elbow plot was nearly flat — WSS dropped only 2.9% from K=1 to K=6. High-dimensional sparse vectors all looked equally distant, so K-means could not separate groups. Rejected.

Approach B — Embeddings + K-means

all-MiniLM-L6-v2 turns each review into a 384-d dense vector that captures meaning. Clusters separated cleanly; silhouette peaked at K=4. Each cluster interpreted with c-TF-IDF keywords + word clouds. Adopted.

Finding 01

Four typical complaint types

The 4,368 complaints fall into four themes — and each airline concentrates in a different one.

ClusterThemenMean sentimentDominant airline
0Gate baggage fees & staff conduct702−0.790Ryanair (96%)
1Refund & cancellation disputes1,355−0.743AirAsia (53%)
2Check-in baggage charges1,052−0.779Wizz (49%)
3Delays & general flight experience1,259−0.753AirAsia (35%)

Distinctive c-TF-IDF keywords per cluster: baggage / staff (0), refund / cancel (1), check-in / pay (2), delay / worst (3). Cluster 0 is Ryanair's "bag sizer at the gate + surprise £55–60 fee" model; Cluster 1 is long, exhausted refund battles (AirAsia's "AVA"/"Bo" chatbots recur).

Finding 02

Each airline is bad in its own way

Composition shows where each airline's complaints land; sentiment shows how harsh those complaints run.

Composition. Ryanair → Cluster 0, Wizz → Cluster 2, AirAsia → Clusters 1 & 3. They are not "bad" in the same way.

Intensity. The baggage-fee clusters (0, 2) run consistently darker — harsher — than the refund/delay clusters (1, 3), across airlines.

Finding 03 · key result

Sentiment splits into two groups, not four

A pairwise t-test of cluster sentiment shows the four clusters collapse into two statistically distinct groups — every cross-group gap is significant at p < .001, every within-group gap is not.

ComparisonΔ meanpDifferent?
0 vs 2−0.0110.217No · same
1 vs 30.0100.175No · same
0 vs 1−0.048<.001***Yes
0 vs 3−0.038<.001***Yes
2 vs 10.036<.001***Yes
2 vs 3−0.027<.001***Yes

Two emotional tiers

Group A · harsher
1,754 (40%)
Group B · milder · 2,614 (60%)

Group A (≈ −0.78 to −0.79): Clusters 0 + 2 — surprise baggage fees, in person, at the gate and check-in desk. Group B (≈ −0.74 to −0.75): Clusters 1 + 3 — async refund disputes and operational delays.

Interpretation: the harshest anger isn't abstract money lost — it's in-person confrontation over surprise fees. Being charged unexpectedly, face-to-face, beats a remote refund fight or a delay.

Recommendation

Fix the high-intensity, low-cost target first

Rank by sentiment intensity ÷ cost-to-solve. That points straight at Group A — the problem is surprise, not the fee itself, and transparency is cheap.

AirlineAct onClusterPriority
RyanairGate baggage transparency (harshest, dominant complaint)0High
WizzCheck-in fee transparency2High
AirAsiaRefund / chatbot trust (longer-term, milder sentiment)1Medium
ScootDiffuse complaints, no single weakness — monitor—Watch

※ Fix is cheap: upfront total-price displays at booking, clear cabin-bag sizing/pricing before the airport, consistent gate/desk signage. None of this cuts the ancillary revenue the LCC model depends on.

Business insight

Conclusions → decisions

The data reframes the original problem. We started from "LCCs collect too many bad reviews" as if it were one undifferentiated wall of anger to be lowered broadly. Clustering shows it is not: the 4,368 complaints split into four themes that, by sentiment, collapse into just two emotional tiers. And the harsher tier is not where the most money is lost or where operations fail worst — it is where customers are charged unexpectedly, in person, at the gate and the check-in desk. That single fact reorders the whole response.

Because the harshest ~40% of complaints (1,754 reviews, clusters 0 + 2) stem from surprise rather than the fee itself, the highest-intensity problem is also the cheapest to solve: make pricing transparent before the airport — upfront total-price displays, clear cabin-bag sizing, consistent gate signage — and the anger largely dissolves without touching the ancillary revenue the low-cost model depends on. The refund (cluster 1) and delay (cluster 3) groups are the opposite trade: milder sentiment, far costlier to fix, so they are the wrong place to spend first. The recommendation is therefore not "improve everything" but a targeted, per-airline transparency fix — Ryanair at the gate, Wizz at check-in — that protects review scores and word-of-mouth at minimal cost. The cards below summarize each decision this drives.

1

Anger ≠ money lost

In-person surprise fees outrank remote refund fights and delays in intensity — target the moment of confrontation.

2

40% of complaints, worst sentiment

The two harsh clusters (0 + 2) are 1,754 of 4,368 reviews — fixing them moves the needle most.

3

Cheap to fix

The problem is surprise, not the fee. Transparency at booking/airport costs communication, not service cuts.

4

Airlines differ

Ryanair, Wizz, AirAsia each concentrate in a different cluster — no blanket fix; act per-airline.

5

Group B is a poorer first target

Refunds (costly CS overhaul) and delays (operationally expensive) carry milder sentiment — defer them.

6

Retention without price cuts

Turning "surprise" into "I knew what I'd pay" protects review scores and word-of-mouth at minimal spend.

Limitations · what would change the conclusion

Where this is soft

Honest caveats — each names what would shift the result if it broke.

A

Low silhouette (0.049)

Clusters exist but aren't sharply separated — expected when all reviews are pre-filtered complaints sharing one semantic.

B

Trustpilot only

Merging Skytrax made clusters worse, so single-source — cleaner but less generalizable across platforms.

C

VADER is rule-based

Not trained on airline text; sarcasm and context can mislead it, so some sentiment scores may be wrong.

D

English-only

Non-English reviews scored unreliably and are effectively out of scope.

Data: 6,460 Trustpilot reviews · 4 LCCs (Wizz, Ryanair, AirAsia, Scoot) · collected via Selenium, updated 2026-05-27. Method: VADER sentiment (< −0.3 kept) → all-MiniLM-L6-v2 embeddings → K-means (K=4) → c-TF-IDF + pairwise t-test. Reproducible: KMeans(random_state=0), UMAP(random_state=42). Limitations: low silhouette, single-source, rule-based sentiment, English-only.
